summary: Gruber reports on major App Store changes announced by Phil Schiller ahead of WWDC 2016. The biggest change is opening subscription-based pricing to all app categories, not just media apps, with an improved 85/15 revenue split after the first year. App Store review times have dramatically improved, with 50% of apps now approved within 24 hours. Apple is also introducing search ads, which Gruber was initially skeptical of but finds acceptable given the privacy-respecting implementation. He argues subscription pricing could finally make indie app development sustainable and enable professional-caliber iPad apps.
key_points: Subscription pricing is now available to all App Store categories, effectively solving the free trial and recurring revenue problems that have plagued developers since 2008
Apple's new 85/15 revenue split after the first year of subscriptions recognizes that developers do most of the ongoing work for successful apps
App Store review times dropped from roughly a week to 50% approved within 24 hours and 90% within 48 hours through tooling, staffing, and policy changes
Search ads are privacy-respecting, single-ad-at-a-time, pay-per-tap placements designed to help small indie developers compete for the 65% of downloads that start with App Store searches
These changes represent more transformation in six months under Schiller's leadership than in the previous eight years of the App Store
money_quotes: I think this is the first time the App Store supports a business model that sustains long-term ongoing development of deep applications.
You might argue that people don't want to subscribe to a slew of different apps. But the truth is most people don't want to pay for apps, period. Nothing will change that.
I would argue that the App Store has changed more in the last six months than it did in the previous eight years.
This could be the change that makes the market for professional-caliber iPad apps possible.
Downloads are already being driven by paid ads, so they might as well be in the App Store itself, where Apple can take some of the money and deliver ads that live up to its standards for privacy.
Paying for each major new version, however, is arguably a relic of the age when software came in physical boxes.
Phil Schiller's enthusiasm for small indie developers is undeniable. It's palpable. The new 85/15 revenue split after the first year of a subscription is proof of it.
key_insight: Subscription pricing for all App Store categories, combined with the 85/15 revenue split, represents Apple's first real answer to the unsustainable economics that have plagued indie app development since 2008.
